comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Once again, it's technically Monday now -- past midnight Eastern time -- and here I am on Dreamwidth, so it's time for another Magic Monday.  

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all.

With that said, have at it! 

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***

comments.title: 2 questions
comments.content:
1). You mentioned ceremonial magic won’t make you rich, thin, famous, etc. What are its benefits? Drawbacks, if any?

2). What exactly is chaos magic and why would people do it? Seems to me there’s enough chaos in the world without people conjuring up even more.

replies.title: Re: 2 questions
replies.content:
1) Every human being has some capacity for greatness, which expresses itself partly in the form of innate talents and partly in the form of a capacity for living fully, richly, and deliberately. Most people never get around to finding their capacities for magnificence, and so lead mediocre lives. High magic, to give it the name Eliphas Levi bestowed on it, is a way to cultivate that capacity for greatness, to live more fully and intentionally, through conscious participation in the wider world of spiritual forces to which human souls belong.

The drawbacks? It's a lot of work; most people won't understand or sympathize with what you're doing; and it deprives you of the right to whine about your life.

2) Chaos magic is an avant-garde branch of popular occultism that was originally inspired by the Discordian scene -- a prankster-ish movement in the 1960s and 1970s that claimed to worship Eris, the Greek goddess of chaos, discord, and confusion. (You can find a decent overview of the Discordian nonscripture, the Principia Discordia, here.) Chaos magic isn't about conjuring up chaos -- it starts from the assumption that the universe is random.

While no two chaos magicians think exactly the same way, the basic assumption of most chaos magicians is that the powers used in magic exist entirely in the human mind, and gods, spirits, symbols, and the like are arbitrary constructs that can be used to focus the magical powers of the individual. It's thus very appealing to people who've bought into the atheism and materialism of today's pop culture. I'm not a fan; it seems like a painfully limiting way to do magic -- but a lot of people seem to find it congenial.

replies.title: Re: 2 questions
replies.content: Thanks, John!

replies.title: Re: 2 questions
replies.content: I think in many cases chaos magic function as a sort of back door in to an enchanted life from materialism. It didn't especially appeal to me, but I know some who moved over time from what you describe to a more spirit centered, animist magical metaphysics, for example Gordon White.

comments.title: Questions on Procedure
comments.content:
I have done and am planning on doing more workings from your Encyclopedia of Natural Magic. I have a couple of questions:

1. With regard to keeping a saucer of vinegar in the house to slowly evaporate and banish magical energies / hostile magic, could this be made more potent or focused by using a vinegar maceration made with protective herbs instead of straight vinegar? I am thinking in particular of chamomile since I am harvesting it at the moment, also rosemary and catnip will be ready soon. Or would this be overdoing it? And should this use of vinegar be short term only? I’ve been keeping a saucer in my bedroom for the past month to clear the air for better sleep. And does the vinegar banish all magical energies or only hostile ones, allowing beneficial, benign energies through?

2. Is it less effective to do workings that involve the energies of planets that are stressed in the natal chart? I have the Sun in his fall (Libra), Venus in her detriment (Scorpio), and Jupiter is retrograde in Taurus. Yet I seem drawn to do workings that involve these planets. Would it be better, more effective to focus on energies of the planets that are better placed in my natal chart?

And last, I have a question about the SOP ritual. I have started doing the first invoking/banishing (Air) and wonder what to do with my arm after I trace the symbol for invoking and before I ‘erase’ the symbol for banishing. Does the arm remain elevated with fingers pointing at the symbol, or should I lower my arm to my side for the visualization / blessings and the statement of gratitude. Then raise it again for the ‘erasing.’ And after the erasing when I am speaking the banishing chant, do I lower my arm to my side or keep it pointing at the now empty space?

This has turned into quite a few questions. Thank you so much for your patience and your instruction. I thoroughly enjoy these Magic Mondays and look forward to them every week.

Yanocoches in Colorado

replies.title: Re: Questions on Procedure
replies.content:
1) You can certainly do magically effective herbal vinegars for this purpose. The vinegar doesn't banish energies, it just keeps them from condensing on the etheric plane, which is what has to happen in most kinds of magical attack; the energies themselves flow freely, as they should.

2) Not at all. One of the benefits of natural magic, in fact, is that you can help counter the negative aspects of your natal chart. Since the chamomile you use, for example, isn't affected by the detriment of the Sun in your natal chart, it radiates a healthy solar energy you don't normally have.

3) With regard to the Sphere of Protection, I point at the center of the symbol while speaking. So I trace, point, trace, point, and then lower the arm. That's not the only way to do it, though; by all means experiment and find the way that works best for you.

comments.title: Tarot numbering
comments.content:
Hi JMG, can you give your view on the different numbering of the Strength and Justice cards, in different decks and how this came about?
Reading some writings on both, they both seem to make sense in their own way.
You are right, too, the Tarot Maconnique is a lovely deck, as is his Alchemical deck

Thanks in advance, Helen

replies.title: Re: Tarot numbering
replies.content:
Okay, this is fairly complex. In the cities of northern Italy, where the tarot evolved, there were many different tarot-ish decks with different numbers and orders of trumps. The Milanese version ended up being exported to southern France, where (after further modifications) it became the Marseilles tarot, and the Marseilles pattern then became standard over most of Europe. It has Justice as trump 8 and Strength as trump 11.

All this happened while the tarot was just a card game. Later on, when card divination became popular, occultists picked it up and found that the trumps made it more useful than other card decks. The big question was how to assign the 22 trumps to the 22 letters of the Hebrew alphabet, which were important in Cabalistic symbolism. There were various systems, each with its strengths and weaknesses.

Then along came a circle of English occultists associated with Kenneth Mackenzie. One of them -- nobody knows who -- rearranged the trumps, moving the Fool from the next to last card to the front of the deck, and swapping Justice and Strength. Once you do that, fitting the trumps to the Hebrew alphabet is a piece of cake. That system got picked up by the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, the most prestigious occult order of late Victorian England. That's where Arthur Edward Waite, the co-designer of the Waite-Smith (or Rider-Waite) tarot deck, learned about tarot, and so when he and Pamela Coleman Smith went to work on what became the most influential tarot deck of modern times, they used the Golden Dawn order of the trumps.

So you have your choice. Both are valid options; it's purely a matter of what you want to do.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: With how thought and intention affect the world, can preparing for worst-case scenarios make them more likely to happen?

replies.title: incomplete
replies.content: You are forgetting the first part of that old Army axiom.  It's "Hope for the best, but prepare for the worst."  Of course, some times things go too well, and you outjump your supply chain.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Think about the words you just used -- "thought and intention." When you prepare for a worst-case scenario, do you intend the scenario to come about? Are you brooding over it, imagining it as vividly as you can, willing it to happen? If so, the answer could well be yes. If not, don't worry about it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
(Tolkienguy)

Hey-thank you for your answer last week. I have been considering trying simple discursive meditation for 15-20 minutes a day with an opening prayer along the lines of "Dear God, please protect me and this space from all harmful spiritual powers.", perhaps combined with a more formal prayer like the Lord's Prayer or the Anglican Collect for Protection. I have no wish to repeat my previous experience but do feel the need for some sort of spiritual practice to bring more...balance and direction into my life.

Also, I ran into David Griffin's website today, and have seen it a couple times since I became interested in the occult, and...is that guy for real? Everything he writes reads like a really, really bad infomercial, but a couple weeks ago I remember you talking about him like he was a serious magician.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
One very traditional way to incorporate discursive meditation into Christian spiritual practice is to read a chapter of the Bible each morning, and then meditate on it. I once had a conversation about this with a very devout Christian occultist, for whom this was a core practice. She'd start with the Lord's Prayer, then read her chapter, then meditate on something in that chapter, then end with a more extempore prayer. It took her about twenty minutes a day and was the mainspring of her spiritual life.

One key piece of advice she offered was never, when you're beginning this practice, start with Genesis. "Even if you make it through the begats," she said, "you're going to grind to a stop in Leviticus or Numbers, and that'll be the end of it." Start with the Gospel of Matthew, proceed from there to the end of the New Testament, and only then go to the first chapter of Genesis and proceed from there.

As for Griffin, well, you get those from time to time...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Don’t know about the other communions, but in the Roman Catholic Church, which dates back to when most people couldn’t afford books, if you go to Mass every day for 3 years you will hear the entire Bible read—except for those notoriously boring sections of Numbers and Leviticus!

comments.title: Preparing the Vervain
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

So I'm doing the second part of my Vervain preparation when I burn the 3ozs and the leftover laceration from the tincture and i had a couple of questions based on the experience:
A) my herbs didn't go gooey or bubbly, but they did go to embers then grey ash, does this mean I missed a part of the process?
B) I have a lot less liquid since I took out the solid matter, do I need to add more alcohol now that I've put the ash in?
Thanks for your steer
Cheers
Edward

replies.title: Re: Preparing the Vervain
replies.content:
a) No, they're fine. If you've got a good gray ash, you have the salts you need.

b) As long as you have enough of the final preparation for the purposes of the ritual, you're fine.

comments.title: Characters in Fiction
comments.content:
About a month ago, you said: "One of the consequences of the way I write things is that I let my characters do the talking, and they routinely say things I'm not expecting. [...] in some cases important characters and major themes come into a story that way, without even so much as asking leave." (ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/20440.html?thread=868312#cmt868312)

This phenomenon is surprisingly often mentioned by competent writers -- for instance, I think it was Isaac Bashevis Singer who credited characters with their own lives and logic which the writer had to adjust to, rather than the other way around. What makes this specifically a subject for Magic Monday is that I have been wondering where these important characters or themes are coming from then: are they just dressed up memories or parts of personality, or something rather more independent of the writer's biography?

By the sound of it, I'd suspect the latter, but it would take someone at home both in the world of fiction writing and of occultism to give a reliable answer to that!

replies.title: Re: Characters in Fiction
replies.content: I have no idea. I haven't wanted to probe the issue too deeply, in case that might cause something to go wrong with the process!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG
I try to answer my own questions – this I guess has long been my own way of meditating. This habit raises new questions, however.

Given that different cultural lineages must negotiate the ‘magical’, where there are autonomies beyond our ken but in some ways available to discovery or rediscovery, I am curious about the lineage of ‘Progress’.

John Mathews (in RC Enlightenment) recounts Tycho Brahe’s last words: “Let me not have lived in vain.” From your knowledge of world cultural traditions / lineages, can we imagine this coming from any other tradition? The striving behind this plea seems so commonplace that I wonder.

And subsequent to this, is what we call ‘Progress’ a degraded version (with added hubris) of something recurrent in a particular cultural soul? Take out the hubris and “we stand on the shoulders of giants”. Where stands this in magical history?

best
Phil H

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Er, maybe I'm just slow on the uptake this afternoon, but I'm not at all sure what you're saying, and even less sure what you're asking.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

For pedagogical purposes, I always present panpsychism (or animism) as the golden middle way between the two Western cosmological extremes of materialism and idealism (Pythagoras as the marriage of Plato and Aristotle), and historically the default theoretical framework for explaining why magic and divination work. A tad simplistic, I know, but would you generally agree? I ask as we're currently seeing an explicit boom in panpsychism among philosophers and even practicing scientists -- few of whom have realized that this venerable cosmology requires magic to be baked into the cake!

A second question, if I may: I'd like to get an earthier sense of how Uranus works, since it's the strongest planet in my chart (in Scorpio in house ten almost conjunct Midheaven). For obvious reasons, of course, no Uranian associations appear in your ENM; but can any of the Saturn-in-Aquarius things -- henbane, jet, obsidian, salt and south wind (Affricus) -- do double duty here? And I suppose technically Mercury-in-Aquarius things might work as well, though I don't see any listed?

Also, I'd like to work with a variety of pines (as an eastern Washingtonian, Ponderosas are my spirit animal), none of which feature in ENM; where do you recommend I go for a reliable list of their correspondences and uses?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Panpsychism -- for those readers who don't have a background in philosophical jargon, this is the belief that mind and consciousness are present in all things -- is to my mind as incomplete as materialism and idealism; it's when you chuck the notion of a single arche in the Greek sense, and note that the universe contains (at bare minimum) material things, minds, and eternal noetic realities, none of which can be reduced to the others, that you get to the place where magicians do their work -- and arguably the universe in which each of us actually lives.

Your second and third questions all deal with areas of magical practice where the necessary work hasn't been done yet. We don't yet know what the herbs and stones of Uranus are; we don't yet know what the elemental and planetary correspondences of New World pines are. Somebody, or more to the point a lot of somebodies over many years, will have to get to work using clairvoyant capacities, interaction with spirits, and experimentation to work all that out.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Re: Uranian Herbs:

For what it's worth, I've had several conversations with a professional herbalist I was working with about this. We speculated that highly diffusive herbs that cause tingling and taste "electric" are likely Uranian in nature.

Lobelia inflata is one such herb, Spilanthes is another. Both are credited with very rapid action. Also, Spilanthes is termed "electric daisy" in one of its common names, which strikes me as extremely Uranian. It causes immense tingling, salivation, and I was introduced it in the context of some very lively gay men who were employing it as a sexual adjunct. This too adds to its Uranian attributions.

In one of the OSS stories I wrote, Spilanthes trafficking plays a key role in the plot of a stranded earthling on Uranus. For those who are curious, the story can be accessed here: http://www.solarsystemheritage.com/the-chthonic-pull.html

comments.title: Mercury in Gemini
comments.content:
Hi JMG

Re the daily divination practice. Would you consider it acceptable to use one of the better astrology websites for my daily natal chart as a gorm of daily divination?

replies.title: Re: Mercury in Gemini
replies.content: Um, your natal chart is the chart you got when you were born; it doesn't change from day to day. If you mean using a site to track transits to your natal chart, you can certainly do that; I don't use the internet for that purpose -- I get the annual ephemeris, and take the few minutes needed to check the day's planetary placements in relation to my natal chart and my wife's -- but either way it's a workable divination practice.

comments.title: Hermetic Herbalism/Natural Magic
comments.content:
Reading Dion Fortune's The Mystical Qabalah and then putting a critical eye towards my gardening books and herbals has gotten me thinking about how the astrological attributions of herbs relate to the Tree of Life. You mention this in The Encyclopedia of Natural Magic as it relates to quartz and the Moon, Yesod etc.

So an herb like Nettles stings, is an aphrodisiac especially for men, strengthens the will, strengthens plants it grows next to and aids in cleansing and catabolism. So clearly this is an herb of Mars, and likewise Geburah.

I would imagine that this sort of mapping of herbs onto the Tree of Life may be done most specifically through the world of Assiah. So it may be fair to say that one of the ways that Geburah/Mars manifests corporally on Earth is through Nettles. In Yetzirah Nettles would exist as an emanation of Mars, in Briah Nettles would be as something like Archetypical patterns and in Atziluth Nettles would be something like a "seed" in the unmanifest.

To contact Mars/Geburah in the world of Assiah the body of Nettles would suffice. However, if I wanted to contact Mars/Geburah in the realm of Yetzirah or 'higher' I would need to make use of Ceremonial techniques, which could include Nettles but would also deal with more abstract forces.

My question is:

does this mapping make sense, is there anything that you see that indicates I'm misunderstanding the concepts and their implications? I'm quite excited by this idea and the utility it might provide -- especially since herbs tend to be not only balanced but even balancing beings, at least in my experience. However I wish to not get too swept up in flights of fancy and am still very new to the Cabala and so seek to see if I'm blatantly not getting something.

Many thanks!

replies.title: Re: Hermetic Herbalism/Natural Magic
replies.content: That's the classic way of thinking of it, so you're obviously paying attention.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Dear HMG, I’ve recently been rereading ‘The White Goddess’ by Robert Graves and was wondering if this book has had any influence on your Druidic practice.?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, in certain ways. I take it for what it is -- the vision of an inspired poet -- and that's valid in its own right, whether or not it has anything to do with what other inspired poets did or didn't do a thousand years ago.

replies.title: The White Goddess
replies.content:
(Deborah Bender)

The White Goddess was a major influence in the primary Craft tradition I practice (NROOGD), and I have spent some time studying it. TWG isn't an easy book to read, owing to the way Graves skips from subject to subject, assumes familiarity with his sources, and makes speculative leaps. For people who want to read it carefully, the closest thing I have found to an Annotated White Goddess is a book called Stalking the Goddess by Mark Carter, Moon Books 2012. Trade paperback from Britain, 15.99 pounds or $26.95.

Carter traces the literary sources of Graves' Celtic material, examines each of the principal themes of the book, evaluates Graves' historical and archeological claims, places the book in historical context and in the context of Graves' life, and so forth. Mr. Carter is a clear writer but a pedestrian one, so this is not an entertaining book, but it is informative.

comments.title: Please Analyze My Spirit
comments.content:
Open Offer to JMG and Other Cosmopolitans That Make Monday Magical:

Please shortly or at length analyze my spirit using my visible planets' natal chart with a view to showing me likely strengths and weaknesses it might manifest if I make being an author one of my top aims.

Sun: Cancer in House 12
Rising: Cancer
Moon: Libra in House 4
Mercury: Gemini in House 12
Venus: Gemini in House 11
Mars: Libra in House 4
Jupiter: Scorpio in House 4
Saturn: Libra in House 4

I'd welcome general comments too. I am Saturn's Pet: behold my innards!

replies.title: Re: Please Analyze My Spirit
replies.content:
Speaking off the top of my head, and without knowing what conjunctions and squares your chart contains, here is a rough outline of what I read:

Born to examine and document end-of-life conditions and places of confinement. This could mean death row prisons, of course, but what I got was the notion that nursing homes are prisonlike. Perhaps written accounts of how to set up nurturing, truly homelike hospices within the structural confines and budgets of nursing homes, including how to staff, finance, and operate the endeavour on a daily basis.

Detailed interviews with the people who are dying, their relatives, their spiritual advisors, the hands-on care-providing staff, and the hands-off, purse-string-holding administrative personnel would be illustrative of the principles
you discover by conducting research into the difficulties of setting up such care units and developing your own insights into how to address the salient issues.

Or you might discover ways to get people out of nursing homes altogether, perhaps by clubbing together to pool resources and share duties of attending on those who require care. People will likely reveal to you deep, intimate, painful, and/or shocking secrets in the course of your
research, showing imbalances in the current system that will cry out for fairer resolution. This source material could be good for sales, or at least spark your creative powers of description and analysis.

You might have to earn your living and beef up your authorial authority by actually being on the spot -- -- living or working in health care facilities in person. Especially if you work in
the accounting dept. and gain inside knowledge of where the money comes from and where it goes.

Another potential topic for writing might be the trials of homelessness and how people manage to go on living when they dwell on the outer edges of survivability. Mental illness is also confining and hidden away from public view. Likewise, rural poverty vs. urban poverty.

replies.title: Re: Please Analyze My Spirit
replies.content:
To This Excellent Anonymous One:

I knew I'd get a gem! You have given gifts. I pray that something useful flows into your cosmos because of this exchange.

Three Thoughts:

1) I am indeed born to examine and document end-of-life-conditions and places of confinement as you thought. This is so weirdly accurate that I suspect you somehow know me offline. lol. Did it all start with an illustration of Morrigan in an old AD&D book?

2) My father and I presently fight a simmering war to keep his parents out of those nightmarish parodies of homes. He's in the trenches for this one and I'm support. We have many tales to tell. I never thought to write about them.

3) I have, however, thought about writing about how my peoples survive de-industrialization in rural Michigan. Your consideration leads me to consider that aim all but confirmed. We're steadily falling apart here in stages except for Amish and Mennonites. What could grow from this decay I wonder?

Begrudgingly Wishing Wholeness For All Beings,

Saturn's Pet

replies.title: Re: Please Analyze My Spirit
replies.content:
LoL, no, I do not know you offline. I was just reading the chart patterns.
Sun (self) and Mercury (writing) in the 12th House,place of end-of-life issues and confinements.
Four planets in the 4th House, place of homelife and natural (unrotated) position for Cancer the Crab:
Moon, unconscious attachments;
Mars, passionate energy and martial spirit;
Saturn, Scythe-carrier, planet of limits and endings;
Jupiter the Expander, associated with joviality, philosophy, publishing.
Three planets in Libra, the sign of justice, balance, and art.
Venus, (love, art, and beauty) in Gemini (communications) in the 11th House, place of friendships and voluntary associations, natural home of Aquarius (universal love).
Virgo, the health-servant sign rotated into the 2nd House, associated with money and possessions;
Capricorn in the 6th House, place of work and service, natural home of Virgo.

Without the degrees and angles, one cannot say whether you are dealing with squares (tension) or inconjuncts (out-of-phase, unrelated) between your your planets in 4th and 12th, though I suspect the Scorpio (sex, death, secrets) Jupiter is in trine with at least one of your 12th house planets.

So it was just a very rough, superficial, amateur reading. A pro could give you much more insight. The bit about nursing homes was me going with my intuition, but I did try to hedge my guess with other possible interpretations.

comments.title: Not a question, but I thought of you when I read this
comments.content:
I am plowing through GK Chesterton's "Heretics" and this little gem bounced out at me. Since I think it fits in nicely with your project, I took the liberty of sending it.


"I do not, therefore, say that the word "progress" is unmeaning; I say it is unmeaning without the previous definition of a moral doctrine, and that it can only be applied to groups of persons who hold that doctrine in common. Progress is not an illegitimate word, but it is logically evident that it is illegitimate for us. It is a sacred word, a word which could only rightly be used by rigid believers and in the ages of faith."

replies.title: Re: Not a question, but I thought of you when I read this
replies.content: Good. He's right, of course, that since the word "progress" implies things becoming better over time, it means nothing without a consensus about what is better and what is worse...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: When you remembered your past lives in meditation were you trying to remember them or were you thinking about something else and the memories came back spontaneously?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It happened very gradually, over a period of some years, and never when I was trying to remember them. In general, if you try to remember past life memories, your imagination will be more than happy to cook something up for you; let it happen in its own time, and you have a better chance of getting something that isn't simply fabrication.

comments.title: Blessing
comments.content:
What is the occult view on blessings? Specifically those done by a priest or priestess. Can the priest channel a spiritual force to do this? Or should blessings be left to the deities and kept out of human hands? I always look forward to this forum. Thanks again for the generosity in providing this space.

Mike T

replies.title: Re: Blessing
replies.content: One of the things that happens when you become a priest or priestess is that you have a link with one or more deities, through which the power and intentions of the deity can take shape more easily in the human world. When the priest or priestess blesses someone or something, power flows through that link from the deity. Of course it depends on how well the priest or priestess keeps the link open via personal spiritual practice; we've all met people who were officially clergypersons, but had no connection with any deity at all and couldn't channel a burnt-out match's worth of spiritual energy...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer (is there an appropriate honorific for a distinguished mage such as yourself other than "mister"?),

I'm grateful for your continued commentary on these subjects, and especially so because I have just started digging into your book on geomancy (and am waiting for the Encyclopedia of Natural Magic) and it's raised a few questions for me!

1. So, I'm in Canada and up here one of the only vendors that I'm aware of selling your books directly is Amazon.ca (the place Llewellyn sends me when I go to their site). You've mentioned that due to the conditions of people working there there may not be such great energy on products they sell. In addition, I was wondering the same about a used magical book. How would you suggest I cleanse used books or those purchased from Amazon once I bring them into my home?

2. You've gone into detail on how vegetarianism increases psychic sensitivity and thus is in some ways incompatible with ritual magic, if I understood your comments correctly. I find that I'm not terribly drawn to ritual magic anyway, but I like the idea of a SOP-type banishing ritual and the ability to perform simple but effective spellwork (along the lines of hoodoo candlework or the effects of using the SOP/LRP for workings). What could I pursue in place of those that might be more appropriate as a druid-minded, celtic/german-ancestry vegetarian?

3. There are some larger-scale life-changes looming on my horizon. I'm a beginner in both tarot and geomancy (thank you for writing such a fantastic book on the subject!). I realize that my ability to correctly formulate questions and parse the answers is still nascent, and I'm concerned about giving both too little and too much weight to the answers I derive from my own divination. How would you recommend I approach these tools with respect to these important decisions and changes? I am somewhat limited in finances or I would consider paying a better regarded mage to do this work on my behalf.

I understand this may be too many questions at once, and I apologize if so. I've really tried to hold back and only ask the most pressing ones!

Thanks again,

D

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
0) I prefer to avoid honorifics altogether; "JMG" is quite sufficient. Still, thank you for asking!

1) If you can, leave the book in sunlight for an hour or so. If that's not an option, burn some frankincense, and fan the book open above the incense so the pages are exposed to the smoke. That should do it.

2) A vegetarian diet with adequate protein shouldn't get in the way of the kind of simple magic you've mentioned, though I'd recommend the SoP rather than the LRP. It's when you do without animal foods altogether that you have to worry about developing crippling sensitivity to other people's energies and emotions.

3) That's why doing daily readings, and keeping track of how they turned out, is so valuable. You'll learn very quickly how to take your readings. Also, of course, basic common sense is one of the most important divinatory powers you can use, and divination works best if you combine it with ordinary reasoning, research, and caution.

comments.title: Druids and Freemasons
comments.content:
Greetings, Archdruid.

The question I have today is about the relation between Druids and Freemasons. I know Druids and Freemasons coexisted together for at least a few hundred years and many Druids were Freemasons as well. I know little about Freemasons but I do know that a belief in a supreme being is a requirement to be a Freemason. There is no supreme being in Revival Druidry, so how can Druids become Freemasons (unless they happen to be Christian Druids)?

I know this question is not strictly about magic, but I hope you will let it slide.

replies.title: Re: Druids and Freemasons
replies.content:
Where did you get the idea that there's no Supreme Being in Revival Druidry? Historically, the great majority of people in the Druid Revival have believed in a supreme being. Remember that "supreme being" does not mean "solitary being"; any pantheon that considers one deity as ruling over the others has a supreme being -- and a great many Druids over the years, for ease of communication, have been willing to use the familiar word "God" for that being. Thus the oldest attested form of the Universal Druid Prayer runs as follows:

"Grant, O God, thy protection,
And in protection, strength;
And in strength, understanding;
And in understanding, knowledge;
And in knowledge, the knowledge of justice;
And in the knowledge of justice, the love of it;
And in that love, the love of all existences;
And in the love of all existences, the love of God and all goodness."

I'm an active Freemason, by the way, and my Druid faith is fairly well known to my lodge brethren; in seventeen years of membership, in four different states, I've never gotten any trouble from Masons about being a Druid.

replies.title: Re: Druids and Freemasons
replies.content:
I am aware that you are a Freemason, I have been following your blogs since 2012 when I first had the pleasure of discovering your works.
However, I have been studying Druidry (alone) for only a year. I have read The Book of Druidry by Ross Nichols, The Mysteries of Britain by Lewis Spence and, naturally, The Druidry Handbook by you.
There is the Druid Prayer and then there is Ceugant plane of existence which implies the existence of God.
But I am (still) confused on the matter, I suppose I will understand this in due time.
I'm glad to hear that Freemasons are accepting of non-Christians.

replies.title: Re: Druids and Freemasons
replies.content: I'm not sure I understand the confusion. You've got the prayer, you've got the concept of Ceugant, so you know there's room for a supreme being in Druidry; since no two Druids believe exactly the same thing about anything, all you have to do is decide how you personally, for the moment, are going to relate to that being, if you choose to do so. Druidry doesn't do dogma!

replies.title: Re: Druids and Freemasons
replies.content:
That's it! Thank you, JMG!

I need to decide that myself. A new topic for meditation.

The lack of dogma was the source of my confusion. It is so different from mainstream religions. But I guess when you deal with the unknowable supreme being, dogma hurts more than it helps.

Once again, you set me on the right track.

replies.title: Re: Druids and Freemasons
replies.content: Oh, I get that. A lot of people find Druidry baffling because it's not something you believe -- it's something you are, and something you do.

comments.title: Faeries and Visualization
comments.content:
Dear JMG, three questions if I may abuse your patience:

1. What's your opinion on the work of RJ Stewart? (particularly the Earth Light/Faerie work) I've always feel a sort of affinity with it (but then, I've always been attracted to European faery traditions...) but at the same time, sometimes I doubt whether the gentleman is truly genuine or just another spiritual salesman...

2. Ditto about Traditional Witchcraft, faery and fetch work, etc... (I'm thinking of the likes of Robin Artisson)

3. In connection with RJ Stewart, he seems to use a lot of (Empowered) Visualization/Pathworking. Just wonder what are the historical origins of this technique so ubiquitous in authors like himself, The Mattthews, etc... and whether it's connected with Ignatius of Loyola's Spiritual Exercises.

Many thanks!
Manuel

replies.title: Re: Faeries and Visualization
replies.content:
1) Stewart's competent and extremely learned. He was a personal student of the late William Gray, and he's worked closely with Gareth Knight from time to time. I can also vouch for the efficacy of his methods -- I worked my way through his Merlin material at one point, and learned a great deal from it.

2) I've never interacted with Robin Artisson, but everyone I know who has done so has had a bad experience. I'd stay well away from anything he's involved in.

3) Good! Yes, Dion Fortune learned a great deal from Loyola's Spiritual Exercises, and most of the current crop of British occultists -- very much including Stewart and the Matthews -- studied with Fortune's students.

comments.title: Mercurius
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Thank you so much for Magic Mondays! Have you read Patrick Harpur's Mercurius and if so what are your thoughts on his discussions concerning Carl Jung and Alchemy?

replies.title: Re: Mercurius
replies.content: I haven't read it. Doubtless I'll get to it one of these days!

comments.title: Cultivating allies on other planes
comments.content:
Good Monday to you.

I am presently trying to cultivate relationships with beings who might assist me in filtering out negative/hostile energy that is being directed at me by persons who are involved with demons. It seems as I become more effective in blocking, this person summons even nastier entities to “help” them. (Looking at the astrology of now, I am expecting the demonic to begin to get quite out of their control over the next few months—oy.)

1. As a Buddhist practitioner, I have actually had a lot of success with requesting aid of buddhas, bodhisattvas and wrathful deities. I suspect there could be other beings out there who might be willing to be allies and I am open to any suggestions you may have for how I might find them!

2. I clear my energy field every night and every morning and occasionally during the day if I notice anything amiss. I also cast protection in my vicinity or home and request assistance with filtering negative energies at those times. These requests for help don’t seem to “stick,” regardless of the duration of assistance I have requested. Should I expect to frequently renew my request for help? (Perhaps this varies per person or per ally.)

3. This seems kind of lop-sided, my asking for help from beings who by their nature are compassionate or for whatever well-intended reason respond to my request for help. It seems I should try to reciprocate something to them. I could try to ask them about this, I suppose, but is this a standard practice when it comes to enjoining other beings for protection? Some sort of propitiation or gift or attempt to reciprocate or show gratitude beyond verbal/emotional thanks?

Thank you in advance for any suggestions and/or pointers to resources!

replies.title: Re: Cultivating allies on other planes
replies.content:
1) Stick with the tradition you know. As a Buddhist, you have a connection with the Buddhist pantheon; you don't have a similar connection with other pantheons, and they may not be interested in helping you.

2) You might want to talk to the beings you're invoking and see what they say. A more experienced practitioner of the kind of Buddhism you practice may also be able to give you some guidance.

3) It depends utterly on the entity in question and the traditions in which they're invoked. Talk to the entities and see what they say.

comments.title: Another bunch of questions :D
comments.content:
Do you know of any good magic-related fiction?

What would you reccommend as the ideal time for doing a daily LBRP, or does it depend on personal convenience?

You often reccomend to master one magical system before trying others. How should I choose what works for me? what should I look for and for how long before I decide to try something else?

Are there magical systems that I can learn from free books? my acquisitive power is limited :(

If I cannot do the three exercises that you often reccomend (Meditation, Divination, and Ritual) which one would be best for me to go with first?

Thanks for your time and attention! It's very kind of you to give us this place for our questions. Juan Pablo (with the waving snail avatar)

replies.title: Re: Another bunch of questions :D
replies.content:
1) You might try Dion Fortune's novels.

2) That's entirely a matter of personal needs.

3) You can't know in advance which system will work for you. All you can do is choose one and see. Once you've made the choice, keep doing it until you've completed the full course of study in the book that you're using.

4) Not that I know of.

5) The three exercises will take you thirty minutes a day. If you can't spare that much time, you're probably too busy to study magic.

replies.title: Re: Another bunch of questions :D
replies.content: Thank you very much. I'm eager to reading about the Cosmic doctrine! I read the first chapter and it was really interesting to me. Especially the notion of geometrically derived morality. Also, like some others that i've seen comment, i find it difficult to picture the right angles of the ring of chaos and the ring of (forgot the name). Will we talk about it in this or the next week?

replies.title: Re: Another bunch of questions :D
replies.content: Yes. I may see if I can make a drawing and include it in the post.

replies.title: Re: Another bunch of questions :D
replies.content: Another thing: i think i have an affinity for the greek pantheon. Do you think i could do the sphere of protection by assigning the four winds to it? I can't find a balanced set of four planetary deities.

replies.title: Re: Another bunch of questions :D
replies.content:
No, you need deities. I'm not sure why you think you need four planetary deities, though! For the Elemental Cross, all you need are two gods, an older and a younger, and two goddesses, ditto; if you can't find those in Greek mythology, er, I don't think you're looking very hard. For the opening of the elemental gates, you need seven deities, four associated with the four elements, and then a mother deity for Spirit Below, a father deity for Spirit Above, and a child deity for Spirit Within; here again, if you can't come up with examples from Greek mythology you aren't giving it an honest try.

Do not make the process harder than it needs to be. I've noticed that people sometimes get caught up in a sort of frenzy of nitpicking, loading on obstacles to what is actually a very simple process. Let's walk through it.

The elemental cross: try Zeus, Apollo, Hera, and Persephone. Simple as that.

The seven elemental stations: Hermes for air, Hestia for fire, Poseidon for water, Demeter for earth. For spirit below, Persephone; for spirit above, Zeus; for spirit within, Dionysos. Again, simple as that.

replies.title: Re: Another bunch of questions :D
replies.content: Thank you very much!

replies.title: Re: Another bunch of questions :D
replies.content:
If I may interject some fiction recommendations here...

On an old post on Galabes, JMG recommended some of the old Weird Tales authors, including Manly Wade Wellman and Seabury Quinn (whose complete stories are recently back in print in some handsome hardcovers).

And speaking of handsome hardcovers, while he may be too modest to mention them again here, I strongly recommend JMG's own Weird of Hali series. (Any word, JMG, on when we might get the next few volumes? I'm eager to find out what happens when "four join their hands where gray rock meets gray tide"!)

replies.title: Re: Another bunch of questions :D
replies.content: The publisher wants to sell a certain number of copies of Kingsport before he's willing to bring out Chorazin. I'm frankly rather discouraged by the slow pace of sales -- though I know it's my own fault for agreeing to have it come out in a fine (and thus expensive) edition.

replies.title: Weird of Hali
replies.content:
(Deborah Bender)

The recent death of Philip Roth prompted a memorial post on Lawyers, Guns and Money. The comments branched into discussion of other American fiction writers. Lovecraft's name came up, and from there, some discussion of other Lovecraftian writers, so I mentioned you. I could not remember the titles so I said that you were writing a series of novels in which the elder gods were the good guys. I said I had not read them but I had read three of your other novels and gave their titles.

Someone was interested enough to look for your Lovecraft novels and reported that a cursory search on the Internet didn't turn them up. So I checked the current form of the Wikipedia article about you; it has a selection of your titles but far from a complete list and no Weird of Hali. I was able to get the title by looking at the sidebars of your blogs but the casual reader wouldn't know about them, and those sidebars only show a few of your recent books. I then went back to the LGM blog and posted the title of the first book in the Weird of Hali series.

I've been meaning to mention this exchange on LGM to you because your writing is your living and it is not easy for a person to find a complete list of the books you have written and edited. One could make a start by typing your name into the Amazon search field, but that would not give the inquirer the full scope of subjects you have written about.

Since there are so many subjects and publishers and some in print and some out, it might be worth the time it would take you to set up a basic website with your books listed in categories, the magazine issues containing your short stories, the anthologies you have edited, your podcasts, etc. all in one place. You would have to update it when a new book comes out or a house drops an old one, but surely this would pay off in increased readership and some of your current readers branching out to subjects they had not previously considered. Ordinarily I would not offer you advice but I want your books to sell.

replies.title: Re: Weird of Hali
replies.content:
Thank you! I appreciate your concern.

But...

First of all, I have no way to edit my Wikipedia page. Wikipedia won't let people edit their own pages, even to correct obvious howlers. There's also a clique of atheists somewhere way up in the Wikipedia hierarchy that go around deleting stuff from pages on living occult authors; a once very complete article on me got deleted and replaced by almost nothing -- though I think there's been some improvement since then. Someone other than me would have to go to work on the page and deal with the baroque complexities of arguing with the rationalists to try to get a fairly complete article with a good list of publications to stay there.

As for a website, I simply don't have the necessary skills, or the time. I've been on several hundred podcasts and online radio broadcasts, for example; I've published well over a hundred short pieces and better than fifty books; it would take many, many hours to get all of that together, and then there's maintenance and the rest of it. If someone wants to do the work, I'd be delighted, but there are only so many hours in a day and mine are about as committed as they can get. I know that probably limits my sales in some areas, but unless someday I can afford to hire a publicity person, my strategy of using word of mouth and blogging to get the word out is probably going to have to do.

replies.title: Re: Weird of Hali
replies.content:
As someone who fought with the atheist clique, it's hard enough to keep articles on people like JMG from disappearing altogether. It's actually been deleted a few times, because apparently the only person who would ever be interested in writing the article is JMG, and they won't let that happen.

Also, they like to move the goalposts whenever anyone finds a source that contradicts what they want to say.

replies.title: Wikipedia
replies.content:
I have added to the bibliography. Will do more as time permits and sort by topic and non-fiction vs. fiction

Rita

Could you give a synopsis (or a good reference) describing what the Cosmic Day, Cosmic Dawn, Cosmic Night, and the like are and/or mean?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: We'll be talking about that in the discussion of The Cosmic Doctrine!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If I may offer the mundane side of things (gotta pair that with one’s spiritual work, you know?)...

How is your time management? Are you often late to things? When you plan activities or tasks, how long do they actually take, and how does that compare to how long you expect them to take (this can really derail a plan)? Do you make deliberate choices of what to do and not do, in order to accomplish the things you have chosen as priorities?

(If you mostly answer yes, good on you! If not, this may be a component of your life that needs work. I notice that people in my life who are especially idealistic or curious can often be hobbled by their overly optimistic but vague plans for how much they can get done in a day. One doesn’t need to go completely opposite and schedule every second, but we are all of us limited mortals, and must make choices about how we spend the time we have.)

comments.title: Undoing the magic of a gift of knives
comments.content:
Hello,

I've been following the sharp object/glamours discussion. I report a pin on a router as decreasing web consumption about 10% in our house with a noted immediate stop on some really annoying web news outlets and cartoons.

As the conversation drifted into people having received knives for gifts, I realized that good friends gave us steak knives as gifts for our wedding...and we've haven't seen much of them since.

I would like to undo that magic and see them more frequently. I can't give them a penny with an explanation of payment, as one commenter mentioned, as that wouldn't go over well.

I also did a divination and got The Moon, reversed, which seems to imply a "lesser degree of deception", which is what I'm proposing... but I'm new at this and welcome input.

I recalled you offered some sweetening relationship methods to others but don't know if that is appropriate for this application.


Thank you for this forum.

replies.title: Re: Undoing the magic of a gift of knives
replies.content: Arrange to visit them at home, and when you can do so without being observed, leave a penny somewhere. It doesn't have to be somewhere obvious. That should do the job.

comments.title: Experimental updates
comments.content:
Re: pins for dispelling iPhone glamour: I have no idea if this is due to the pins I taped to my phone, and, if so, if it's a magical effect or a material one (via interference with...electromagnetic things that I do not understand, or something), but my phone, which has functioned well for years and displayed no issues, all of a sudden burnt out its charging cord, which also damaged the charging port, making it very difficult to charge/keep charged, so now it's reserved for emergency use only. This is an escalation--I had almost immediately experienced a disinterest in the phone and noticed some glitchiness in the touchscreen while typing after placing the pins. I have now removed the pins, since I am out of state and about to start a new job, and I don't want to lose communications entirely!

Re: mitigating the deadening effects of fossil fuels and their derivatives: I decided to focus on my glasses (plastic lenses in plastic frames, worn constantly from waking till sleep). Obviously I can't really cover them with anything, but I wanted to focus on them because I thought it would be easier to detect an enlivening of my vision than a broader sense of vague deadness/aliveness. I daresay you will shake your head at my grab-bag of attempts, but here is what I tried. I tried breathing on them periodically (breath being associated with life; "inspiration") and rubbing tea tree oil on the frames (no great magical thought went into this--I just happened to have some, and it came from a plant, so what the heck). I also tried sweeping a cluster of living pine needles across the lenses. If any of this did anything, I couldn't really tell. However, I happened to find some pine resin seeping down a trunk, and rolled up a couple balls of it (it's kind of like soft wax in texture) and pressed them into the area around the hinges of my glasses, where it wouldn't stick to things but would sit to either side of my eyes. I actually do think I detected an enlivening of my vision afterward--my eyes were caught especially by the motions of birds in flight, trees moving in the wind, flecks of mica sparkling in the soil--all things I had failed to notice or pay attention to previously now caught and held my eyes.

replies.title: Re: Experimental updates
replies.content: Oh, and another data point: You had asked last week where I felt each of the gates respond during the SoP, and I said that I felt water in my upper legs. Last night I was lying in my truck visualizing myself performing the SoP on the inner planes, since it was somewhat awkward to perform it physically while surrounded by campers. I got pretty into the water visualization, feeling the seaweed brushing against my calves in the surf, seeing the porpoises and their calves dipping in and out of the waves and the silvery fish flash by underwater, etc.--almost more of a daydream. All of a sudden I felt the energy sort of break through and rush down my legs all the way into my ankles and feet; my lower legs were left with an icy-hot tingling sensation for some time afterward. So revise my answer to include legs in their entirety for the blue gate! I don't know if this was similar to the energy release I accidentally triggered in my spinal channel by humming during the SoP, or something different, but it was certainly interesting!

replies.title: Re: Experimental updates
replies.content: Most interesting. The commenter who asked about the magical effect of pine trees now has an additional data point. With regard to the SoP, see what happens as you continue the practices; you may find that all the elements eventually spread their effect through your entire body.

replies.title: Re: Experimental updates
replies.content: I shall certainly continue with the SoP; it has already had myriad interesting and beneficial effects, and I am just now getting ready to add the fourth gate! It makes me laugh now to think of all the magic I wanted to tackle in short order when I was first reading your Druid Magic Handbook after joining the AODA, considering the amount of time and effort I am having to put into unfolding this one simple ritual. It seems like about 5% of the work is in performing the ritual itself, and 95% is in dealing with various avoidant tricks of my subconscious and in processing the numerous odd sensations and phenomena evoked by the ritual. It is very rewarding!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi there,

How can someone just starting with the occult discover what
their own capacity for magnificence is?


Thank you for your time.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
By getting to work on the basic practices -- ritual, meditation, and divination -- and letting their capacity for magnificence ripen naturally.

The work of magic is a lot like growing a tree from a seed. You don't have to know, and it doesn't help to know, exactly what kind of tree it is; you simply have to plant it in good soil, and water and tend it regularly, and it will unfold its own innate potential in its own proper time.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Do you believe in soulmates?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Nope. It used to be a really popular pickup line, and before that, it functioned mostly as an excuse for two people who had the hots for one another to ignore little things like who was married to whom. (Dion Fortune has some tart things to say about that in one of her essays.) There will always be some potential partners with whom you resonate, for reasons that are as much magical as material; there will always be some potential partners with whom you've had various interactions in previous lives, and the karma from those interactions may push you into a relationship this time around -- but the notion that this or that person was destined from the dawn of time to be your One And Only? Nope.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
To piggyback,

How common do you suppose it is to run into an individual who you've encountered in a previous life? Does this happen rarely, half a dozen or so people per life? Dozens per life? I realize there is no way to know for sure, but maybe someone who is sensitive to these things has an idea.

And how common to encounter a soul again and again, lifetime after lifetime?

Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's fairly common for souls to run in loose groups, bound together by karmic ties of various kinds, until the karma's been worked out. How many are in such a group varies wildly from case to case, as does the number of lives it takes before all the permutations have been run through and new ties take their place.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I'm looking for suitable farm land with living space and it's very difficult to find at the moment, but still possible. I picked up geomancy from your book a few years back, then dropped it, and now I'm getting back into your Golden Dawn version.

The knife under the router thing has inspired me to try more ritual magic. What would you recommend for finding work/living space?

Also do you have any guidelines on phrasing questions for geomancy? Sometimes I think I get mediocre results because my questions aren't very good, but on the other hand being overly specific can cut off avenues and possibilities in a situation that the reading might otherwise flush out.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Ritual magic doesn't work that way. It's not something you pick up for a specific purpose, the way you'd pick up a saw or a drill; it's something you make part of your life on a daily basis, because that's what it takes to get to the point at which you can reliably do things in your life.

Different people find different ways of phrasing questions more suitable than others. The best way to figure out what works for you is to record all your geomantic divinations and see which questions got the clearest results.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Ok so to extend the metaphor it's more like learning the fundamentals of architecture in order to draw blueprints.

I'll keep up the practice then. Thanks for your answer!

comments.title: The knife experiment
comments.content:
I've been behind on pretty much everything, including reading through your posts here and at ecosophia. Finally got my Army ship date, and I'm leaving for basic training July 2. But I still go through each and every one of your posts, and I was overjoyed when I found the experiment going on with the open blade under a television.

I placed a small toothpick knife (actually like the same picture used in your original post) open under the tv when it was already off. Both of my parents have terrible television watching habits, which will not lessen no matter what I tell them.

Well, yesterday, my mother who always works downstairs with the tv on as background noise was working without the tv on for the first time in a while!

But my dad, who has a lot of time off work now and no hobbies, watches television uninterrupted. Today my mother turned on the tv, but not for a while after coming home. I have no doubt my father will continue to watch unabated, but this seems promising at least for my mother.

Not really a question for Magic Monday, but magic-related so I hope you will ignore my lack of questions :p

Daniel

replies.title: Re: The knife experiment
replies.content: Many thanks for the data point. Best wishes for basic training!

replies.title: shipping out to basic
replies.content: Get a *GOOD* pair of running shoes and put several miles on them before shipping.  That's one of the things I wish I'd done back then.

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
Good night Mr. Greer,

I'm having some difficulty with the figures and their opposites.
Even though I can sense the polarities on Puer/Puella or Via/Populus I find it harder when it comes for example to Fortuna Major/ Fortuna Minor.

In your book The Art and Practice of Geomancy, page 141, you say:

"...The Judge, Fortuna Major, summing up the day, describes it as a period of swift change caused by outside forces, more fortunate than otherwise, and quickly over..."

But aren't "swiftness", "outside help", "rapid or quickly change" the very characteristics of Fortuna Minor?

Cordially,

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: Good. I was wondering whether anyone else caught that. It's a mistake, of course.

comments.title: SOP on a boat
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

1) I have an extended sea voyage coming up, and I wondered what the traditional lore has to say about performing daily banishing, meditation, and divination while on a boat?

2) I was also very impressed with the Buddha statue at the RISD museum mentioned a while back. It intrigued me that in your opinion it had been consecrated expertly. What do you think are the ramifications of taking an object like that out of its context? I like to frequent museums as much as possible, but I am starting to get uncomfortable with sacred objects being treated as trophies. As I recall, the Buddha at RISD was right next door to an Egyptian sarcophagus

Thanks as always for the opportunity to ask these questions!

Samurai_47

replies.title: Re: SOP on a boat
replies.content:
The traditional lore doesn't have much to say about doing magic on a boat, so you're about to engage in original research. Keep detailed notes on the results you get!

As for sacred objects in museums, a lot depends on what kind of sacred objects they are. The statue of Vairocana Buddha at RISD was made for display in a public temple, and people come there to sit and meditate in its presence from time to time, so the powers that work through it seem fairly content. Other sacred things are not supposed to be public, and I wouldn't want to spend too much time in a museum that displayed them openly!

replies.title: Sacred objects in museums
replies.content:
Despite my interest in anthropology and having taken courses in African and Oceanic art, I avoid those areas of museums. I'm not really very sensitive to 'vibes' but many of the sacred masks, figures of deities and associated objects just seem to be screaming 'get away.' I was especially outraged by an exhibition of objects from Vatican collection that included human skulls prepared and decorated for veneration as ancestors. Oddly, I don't get creeped out by Egyptian mummies. Maybe the ka has been left behind in Egypt, or the soul has moved on to new incarnations and is even advanced to the point of being amused at the notion of living forever in the tomb. Who knows.

Rita R

comments.title: New Question
comments.content: What, if anything, do you think individual U. S. residents can do to improve the terrible egregor there?

replies.title: Re: New Question
replies.content: Regular banishing rituals will help, but ultimately things will change when either enough people stop feeding the nasty egregor, on the one hand, or war or calamity releases the nasty energies on the other. I suspect it'll be the latter choice, but we'll see.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Is it necessary to perform a banishing before meditation or to meditate in the same space where an earlier banishing has been performed?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's not necessary, but it's helpful to banish immediately before meditating.

comments.title: Fear
comments.content:
Archdruid,

I'm having real trouble understanding part of Atkinson's and Charles Godfrey Leland's works on will power. Both authors repeatedly admonish their readers to reject fear because it inhibits the designs of the mage. I have a different experience with fear, for me fear has always been a force that drives me to greater accomplishment. It is a part of me, but both authors say that it is a bad thing. The same goes with doubt. I would never have been able to accomplish the various amazing things that I have, without fear and doubt. What do I do with this contradiction?

Regards,

Syfen

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content: Leland and Atkinson are writing for the masses, and there will always be people for whom some of their details don't apply. If you have something that works, use it. If you aren't getting the results you want from your methods, though, consider giving theirs a try.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: With respect to the question about an introductory book on Western astrology, posting the question on an astrology forum might produce some good results. If I had to recommend one book, I think it would by Myrna Lofthus, A Spiritual Approach to Astrology. I just checked, and it is still in print. I have an older edition, but the reviews on the one that's out there now are pretty good.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I am left-handed. Does this have any efffect on how I should perform rituals? I am working through Learning Ritual Magic.

Best regards,
Olof

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Nope. I'm left-handed too, and I still do everything the standard way.

comments.title: Pins on Televisions and phones
comments.content:
An observation if I may: following the knive under television and/or pins on phones experimenting and data-collecting this stuck out to me: there are some for whom the device apparently seems to go into malfunction or disfunction and some from whom it cuts usage or does nothing at all. the last group as JMG has mentioned might be onto the device for other reasons.

I wonder if there might be an underlying pattern in the difference between mal/disfunction and cutting of usage, as in: is it maybe the difference in intention while casting the spell? Is it the difference in accompanying the placing with/out the spell? Is it how much will can be put behind the spell by the spellcaster - as in the more hard work has gotten into developing the magical practice the lighter one should go whilst placing the spell/pinty thingy?

Delighted that this is an ongoing research, thank you JMG for holding a space for this.
